  • Sophia Wu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Caden Wang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U17/XD (3 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Justin Kan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #5)

Jaleesa has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Jaleesa has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Jaleesa brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jaleesa_yu.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Jaleesa Yu, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 47 career entries in 17 tournaments, Jaleesa has 3 titles — a 6% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 14 entries where Jaleesa drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 47 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jaleesa saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2022 SoCal Arena CRC in GD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jaleesa's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 2 titles in 17 entries — a stretch where Jaleesa actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Jaleesa is 0-for-12. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jaleesa plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Jaleesa is 0-for-13. Zero titles in 13 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Jaleesa and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Jaleesa needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Jaleesa has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jaleesa's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Prutha Bharadwaj Ramaprakash: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Prutha Bharadwaj Ramaprakash has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U15 — we hope Jaxton Zhang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Jaleesa's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jaleesa entered 9 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jaleesa's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jaleesa and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jaleesa has shown up 2 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Jaleesa has also played up into older age groups at 3 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jaleesa is not just losing locally — Jaleesa is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yu family travels cross-country so Jaleesa can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jaleesa has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jaleesa showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in GS U15, which means Jaleesa traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Jaleesa entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Jaleesa paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Jaleesa's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 3 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 44 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Jaleesa is a player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. Will Jaleesa figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Jaleesa's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

james_lai.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the James Lai file just landed on our desk. James hails from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. James has 5 career entries across 3 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The James saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. James plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 5 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

At Junior Nationals, James has shown up 2 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. James has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where James showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 25th-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U19, which means James traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where James entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 25th. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying James paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, James keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities James has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without James, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

janice_chen.name: Janice Chen
janice_chen.summary:
Hailing from California, Janice Chen is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist, earning a podium finish representing the USA against the best young players in the Western Hemisphere (XD U15). A top-3 finish at the Pan Am Juniors puts Janice in rarified international company.

Janice is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (5 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Janice competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 6 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior bronze medalist (XD U15, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event top-3 (U15 XD, U13 GD, U15 GD) • Junior Nationals champion (GD U13) • 5 ORC titles.

The numbers tell a compelling story: Janice boasts a commanding 71% win rate across 86 matches, a clutch 63% win rate in three-game matches (12/19), a biggest win margin of 37 points.

Janice has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 17 different players across the career:
  • Stephanie Jiayi Song (current primary partner) — 12 events together this season alone with 3 titles, building serious momentum (26 total events in GD/U13/U15, spanning 5 seasons)
  • Mikael Chang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U15/XD (17 events, best: #2, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Jayden Mar — 2 events in XD, best: #3, across 2 seasons

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 2 titles across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Janice is still ascending.

Janice has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Janice has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Janice has been seeded #1 or #2 in 5 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 7 titles, Janice is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
janice_chen.roast:
Allow us to introduce Janice Chen, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Janice is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist (XD U15), which means Janice has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Janice's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. But here is the thing about a bronze medal: it means Janice went all the way to an international championship and STILL did not win. Janice represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 101 career entries and a 7% win rate, the Pan Am bronze is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 101 entries worth of material.

The Janice saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2023 Dave Freeman Jr OLC in GS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Janice's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 3 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Janice actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 16 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Can we talk about Janice and Girls' Singles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 4 finals, 3 losses. Janice can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Girls' Singles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Janice still cannot reach it. Can we talk about Janice and Mixed Doubles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 5 finals, 5 losses. Janice can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Mixed Doubles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Janice still cannot reach it. Can we talk about Janice and Girls' Doubles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 7 finals, 1 losses. Janice can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Girls' Doubles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Janice still cannot reach it.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Janice experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Janice has reached 16 championship matches across this career. That is 16 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 16 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Janice entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (GS U11, XD U11, GD U11). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (4 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (12 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Janice does own 7 actual titles, so it is not like Janice has never won anything. But 16 finals losses versus 7 wins means Janice is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Janice plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 6 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Janice can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Janice has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Janice's texts. The most successful partnership is with Stephanie Jiayi Song — 26 events, 6 titles — and honestly, Stephanie Jiayi Song deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Stephanie Jiayi Song a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U13 — we hope Jonathan Wu has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Janice entered 9 events with results ranging from top-3 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Janice's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Janice and said 'nah.' Then there is the seeding situation. Janice has been seeded in 17 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Janice takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U13, and finishing 5th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Janice. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Janice of is playing it safe. Janice has entered older age groups at 5 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17 — which means Janice has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 7 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Janice has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Janice is not just losing locally — Janice is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chen family travels cross-country so Janice can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Janice has 16 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Janice showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U15, which means Janice traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Janice entered 3 events with results ranging from 4th to 65th. That is an average placement of 24th, which is a polite way of saying Janice paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Janice story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 7 titles across 5 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who has turned Girls' Singles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning. But every season Janice comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Janice, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Japjit has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Japjit showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC in GS U15, which means Japjit traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Japjit is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Japjit wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

jasmine_cheung.roast:
Allow us to introduce Jasmine Cheung, from New York, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 17 events across 7 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 17-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Jasmine's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Jasmine keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Jasmine saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jasmine plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 17 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Jasmine has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Edward Chang (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Edward Chang has emotionally recovered.

Jasmine has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jasmine has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jasmine showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Jasmine traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Jasmine entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Jasmine paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Jasmine keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Jasmine has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Jasmine knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.

jasmine_la.name: Jasmine La
jasmine_la.summary:
Jasmine La is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist — representing the United States on the international stage and coming home with gold. Winning at the Pan Am Junior Championships (GD U13) is an elite, continent-level achievement. With 3 career titles across 10 tournaments, this is a player who delivers on the biggest stages.

Jasmine is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 3 doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior champion (GD U13, 2019) • U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U19 XD, U17 GD) • 1 ORC title.

With 7 different doubles partners over the years, Jasmine adapts to anyone:
  • Vivian Shi (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U19 (5 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Vaughn Zhao (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U19/XD (3 events, best: #4)
  • William Ye — a trusted U17/XD partner (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Jasmine has competed across U13, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jasmine has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 3 titles, Jasmine is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
jasmine_la.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Jasmine La file just landed on our desk. Jasmine hails from parts unknown, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Jasmine is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist (GD U13), which means Jasmine has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Jasmine's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. A Pan Am gold medal. International champion. The real deal. We would love to end the story right here, but unfortunately there are 21 career entries to discuss and a 14% overall win rate that suggests the Pan Am gold was the peak of a very pointy mountain with a LOT of valley on either side. Let's dig in.

The Jasmine saga began at 2019 Pan Am Junior, where Jasmine burst onto the scene with a title in GD U13 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Jasmine has delivered on some of those promises: 3 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Jasmine promised and what Jasmine has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2018-2019 season — 2 titles in 2 entries — a stretch where Jasmine actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Jasmine is 0-for-6. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Take away Girls' Doubles and Jasmine has never won anything. Not once. 13 entries across other disciplines, zero titles. Jasmine is essentially a one-club golfer who showed up to a tournament with 18 holes and one iron.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Jasmine experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Jasmine has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Jasmine does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Jasmine has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 3 wins means Jasmine is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jasmine plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Jasmine is 0-for-1. Zero titles in 1 tries. The best singles result is a 9th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Jasmine and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Jasmine needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Jasmine has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Vivian Shi: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Vivian Shi has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U17 — we hope William Ye has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jasmine entered 6 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Jasmine's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jasmine and said 'nah.'

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jasmine is not just losing locally — Jasmine is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 14% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The La family travels cross-country so Jasmine can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jasmine has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jasmine showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U17, which means Jasmine traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Jasmine entered 2 events with results ranging from 1st to 33rd. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Jasmine paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 3 titles, 21 entries, and 18 that ended without a trophy. A player who chokes in finals like it is a paid hobby. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the moments of brilliance — Jasmine looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Jasmine. We will be watching.

jasmine_yeung.name: Jasmine Yeung
jasmine_yeung.summary:
Hailing from California, Jasmine Yeung is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist — representing the United States on the international stage and coming home with gold. Winning at the Pan Am Junior Championships (GD U13, GD U19, Women's Singles, GD U11, GS U13, GS U11, Mixed Doubles, XD U19, XD U11) is an elite, continent-level achievement. With 36 career titles across 23 tournaments, this is a player who delivers on the biggest stages.

Jasmine is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Jasmine especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 4 singles titles, 15 doubles titles, 11 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior champion (GD U13, GD U19, Women's Singles, GD U11, GS U13, GS U11, Mixed Doubles, XD U19, XD U11, 2017, 2019, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event champion (U15 XD, U19 XD, U15 GS, U17 XD, U19 GD, U17 GD, U15 GD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (XD U17, XD U19, GD U19) • 17 ORC titles.

Jasmine has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 13 different players across the career:
  • Angela Ye (current partner) — an ongoing partnership that keeps producing results: 6 titles across 7 events in GD/U15, spanning 3 seasons
  • Zicheng Xu (current partner) — an ongoing partnership that keeps producing results: 2 titles across 4 events in XD, across 2 seasons
  • Audrey Chang — 5 events in GD/U19, producing 5 titles together
  • Arden Q Lee — 4 events in Mixed/U19/XD, producing 2 titles together

Jasmine has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jasmine has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Jasmine has been seeded #1 or #2 in 5 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 7 seasons and 36 titles, Jasmine has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
jasmine_yeung.roast:
Allow us to introduce Jasmine Yeung, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Jasmine is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist (GD U13, GD U19, Women's Singles, GD U11, GS U13, GS U11, Mixed Doubles, XD U19, XD U11), which means Jasmine has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Jasmine's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. A Pan Am gold medal. International champion. The real deal. We would love to end the story right here, but unfortunately there are 70 career entries to discuss and a 51% overall win rate that suggests the Pan Am gold was the peak of a very pointy mountain with a LOT of valley on either side. Let's dig in.

The Jasmine saga began at 2017 Pan Am Junior, where Jasmine burst onto the scene with a title in GS U11 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Jasmine has delivered on some of those promises: 36 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Jasmine promised and what Jasmine has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 9 titles in 15 entries — a stretch where Jasmine actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 3 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Now we arrive at the part of the program that Jasmine's therapist probably knows by heart: the finals record. Brace yourself. Jasmine has reached 9 championship matches across this career. That is 9 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 9 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 South ORC, where Jasmine entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (GS U17, XD U17, GD U19). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (6 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Jasmine does own 36 actual titles, so it is not like Jasmine has never won anything. But 9 finals losses versus 36 wins means Jasmine is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jasmine plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 4 singles, 15 doubles, 11 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jasmine can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Jasmine has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jasmine's texts. The most successful partnership is with Jun Heng Lai — 10 events, 8 titles — and honestly, Jun Heng Lai deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Jun Heng Lai a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting.

Then there is the seeding situation. Jasmine has been seeded in 11 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Jasmine takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in GS U15, and finishing 3rd. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Jasmine. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Jasmine of is playing it safe. Jasmine has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Jasmine has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 31 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Jasmine has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jasmine is not just losing locally — Jasmine is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 51% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yeung family travels cross-country so Jasmine can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jasmine has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jasmine showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U17, which means Jasmine traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U19. A pattern, not a blip.

The Jasmine story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 36 titles across 7 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who has a finals record that should come with a trigger warning. But every season Jasmine comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Jasmine, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

jason_gu.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Jason Gu, from New York, continues to exist, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 82 career entries in 31 tournaments, Jason has 11 titles — a 13% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 6 entries where Jason drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 82 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jason saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 2 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2023 NE OLC (NVBC) in BS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jason's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 5 titles in 22 entries — a stretch where Jason actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 4 wins in 22 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

In Mixed Doubles, Jason has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 4 championship appearances, 4 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Jason chokes in Mixed Doubles finals specifically.

Now we arrive at the part of the program that Jason's therapist probably knows by heart: the finals record. Brace yourself. Jason has reached 9 championship matches across this career. That is 9 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 9 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 NE MassBad OLC, where Jason entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U15, XD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (4 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (5 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Jason does own 11 actual titles, so it is not like Jason has never won anything. But 9 finals losses versus 11 wins means Jason is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jason plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 7 singles, 4 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jason can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 23 of them, to be precise. Jason has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jason's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Iris Kuan: 12 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 12 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Iris Kuan has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Iris Kuan has emotionally recovered.

Of Jason's 11 career titles, 9 came at local events versus 2 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Jason feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jason entered 3 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Jason's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jason and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Jason 7 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any. Then there is the seeding situation. Jason has been seeded in 21 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Jason takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15, and finishing 9th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Jason. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Jason of is playing it safe. Jason has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17 — which means Jason has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 11 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Jason has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jason is not just losing locally — Jason is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 13% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Gu family travels cross-country so Jason can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jason has 23 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jason showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13, which means Jason traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Jason entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Jason paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Jason story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 11 titles across 4 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who the undisputed champion of second place in Mixed Doubles. But every season Jason comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Jason, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

Representing New York, Jason H Chuck is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U17 level, with 10 entries across 4 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

A true triple-threat, Jason competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Jason has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 5 different players across the career:
  • Aidan Wu (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Jason has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Jason brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jason_h_chuck.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Jason H Chuck, from New York, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 10 events across 4 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 10-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Jason's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Jason keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Jason saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jason plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 10 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Jason has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) in BD U13 — we hope Vishanth Vijay has emotionally recovered.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jason has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jason showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) in BS U13, which means Jason traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13), where Jason entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Jason paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Jason keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Jason has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Jason, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

jason_yang.name: Jason Yang
jason_yang.summary:
Out of California, Jason Yang is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-3 finish at Selection places Jason in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

What makes Jason especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-3 (U13 BS, U13 BD) • Junior Nationals competitor (8 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Jason has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Jason has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Jason brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jason_yang.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Jason Yang, from California, continues to exist, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-3. Top-3 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 49 career entries in 18 tournaments, Jason has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 48 entries where Jason drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 49 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jason saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Jason would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 9 times Jason has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 9 times Jason has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Jason's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jason plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 49 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Jason has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jason's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Allen w Yang: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Allen w Yang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U13 — we hope Vivian Feng has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jason entered 3 events with results ranging from top-3 to a humbling round-of-5. The Selection committee looked at Jason's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jason and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jason has shown up 8 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jason is not just losing locally — Jason is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yang family travels cross-country so Jason can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jason has 25 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jason showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U13, which means Jason traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Jason entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Jason paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

5 seasons. 49 entries. Zero titles. If this were a stock, the SEC would have delisted it by now. If this were a restaurant, the health inspector would have questions. If this were a dating profile, the app would gently suggest 'trying a different approach.' But this is junior badminton, where hope springs eternal and so does Jason's entry form. We salute the commitment. We question the strategy. We will absolutely be back next season to see if anything changes. (It will not.)

Representing Pennsylvania, Jason Yiheng Luo is a tenacious competitor with 3 quarterfinal-or-better finishes, currently competing at the U17 level, building a strong foundation across 9 tournaments. Deeper runs are on the horizon.

Jason is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Jason especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

With 11 different doubles partners over the years, Jason adapts to anyone:
  • Lucas Liu (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Justin Dong (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Jason has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Jason's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
jason_yiheng_luo.roast:
Step right up and meet Jason Yiheng Luo, from Pennsylvania, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Jason has 24 career entries across 9 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Jason saga began at 2024 NE NJBC CRC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jason plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 24 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Jason has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jason's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Erela Liu has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Jason has shown up 3 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

One thing nobody can accuse Jason of is playing it safe. Jason has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17 — which means Jason has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' The results in those older brackets have been... character-building. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Jason has built a permanent residence on that line. Jason has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jason has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jason showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U15, which means Jason traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Jason entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Jason paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Jason keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Jason has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Jason, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

jason_zhu.name: Jason Zhu
jason_zhu.summary:
Hailing from California, Jason Zhu has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U13 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Jason's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Jason has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Jason competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (8 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

Jason has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 13 different players across the career:
  • Leo Ye (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events)

Jason has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Jason has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Jason brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jason_zhu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jason Zhu, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Jason has 39 career entries across 15 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Jason saga began at 2023 SoCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jason plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 39 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Jason has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jason's texts. The go-to partner is Hannah Luyi Xiao (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Leo Ye has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Jason has shown up 8 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Jason has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jason is not just losing locally — Jason is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhu family travels cross-country so Jason can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jason has 22 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jason showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Jason traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Jason entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 41st, which is a polite way of saying Jason paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 39 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Jason. Jason just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Jason has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Jason. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Jason: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

jasper_chen.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Jasper Chen, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 17 events across 8 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 17-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Jasper's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Jasper keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Jasper saga began at 2022 Dave Freeman OLC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Jasper would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Jasper has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Jasper has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Jasper's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jasper plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 17 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. The longest-running partnership is with Weirui Yu: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Weirui Yu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U17 — we hope Weirui Yu has emotionally recovered.

Jasper has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jasper has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jasper showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U17, which means Jasper traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Jasper entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Jasper paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Jasper keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Jasper has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Jasper knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.
