Out of California, Jaxton Zhang has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Jaxton's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Jaxton has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Jaxton especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Jaxton has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 9 different players across the career:
  • Jaleesa Yu — a trusted XD partner (4 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Jaxton has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jaxton has competed across 4 different regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Jaxton brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jaxton_zhang.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Jaxton Zhang, from California, continues to exist, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Jaxton has 25 career entries across 12 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 8% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Jaxton saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2022 SoCal Arena CRC in BS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jaxton's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 11 entries — a stretch where Jaxton actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Jaxton is 0-for-6. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Boys' Doubles finals and Jaxton — name a more iconic tragedy. 2 trips to the championship match, and Jaxton has turned 1 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Jaxton's trophies to save time. Can we talk about Jaxton and Boys' Singles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 2 finals, 1 losses. Jaxton can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Boys' Singles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Jaxton still cannot reach it.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Jaxton experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Jaxton has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Jaxton does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Jaxton has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 2 wins means Jaxton is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jaxton plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jaxton can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Jaxton has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jaxton's texts. The go-to partner is Jaleesa Yu (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U15 — we hope Jaden Ke Hong Chen has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Jaxton's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Jaxton has shown up 3 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Jaxton has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jaxton is not just losing locally — Jaxton is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 8% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Jaxton can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jaxton has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jaxton showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in BS U15, which means Jaxton traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Jaxton entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Jaxton paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Jaxton story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 2 titles across 3 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who has turned Boys' Doubles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning. But every season Jaxton comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Jaxton, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

jay_chun.name: Jay Chun
jay_chun.summary:
A Washington competitor, Jay Chun is a Pan American Junior Championships silver medalist, earning a podium finish representing the USA against the best young players in the Western Hemisphere (Men's Singles). A top-2 finish at the Pan Am Juniors puts Jay in rarified international company.

Notably, Jay has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Jay especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 5 singles titles, 3 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior silver medalist (Men's Singles, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U17 BD, U17 BS) • Junior Nationals champion (BD U17) • 4 ORC titles.

Jay has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 11 different players across the career:
  • David Zuo (current partner) — an ongoing partnership that keeps producing results: 2 titles across 14 events in BD/U17/U19, spanning 5 seasons
  • Yaorui Shan — 3 events in XD, best: #3
  • Coco Cenke Tao — 2 events, 1 title together in XD

Jay is peaking at the right time in 2025-2026, with 1 title across 1 tournaments — a clear sign that Jay is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Jay has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jay has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Jay has been seeded #1 or #2 in 5 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 5 seasons and 9 titles, Jay has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
jay_chun.roast:
Clear your schedule, because we need to talk about Jay Chun, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Jay is a Pan American Junior Championships silver medalist (Men's Singles), which means Jay has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Jay's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. But here is the thing about a silver medal: it means Jay went all the way to an international championship and STILL did not win. Jay represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 46 career entries and a 20% win rate, the Pan Am silver is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 46 entries worth of material.

The Jay saga began at 2021 NW CRC, where Jay burst onto the scene with a title in XD U15 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Jay has delivered on some of those promises: 9 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Jay promised and what Jay has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 5 titles in 21 entries — a stretch where Jay actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 2 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Boys' Doubles finals and Jay — name a more iconic tragedy. 4 trips to the championship match, and Jay has turned 1 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Jay's trophies to save time.

Now we arrive at the part of the program that Jay's therapist probably knows by heart: the finals record. Brace yourself. Jay has reached 7 championship matches across this career. That is 7 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 7 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Jay does own 9 actual titles, so it is not like Jay has never won anything. But 7 finals losses versus 9 wins means Jay is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jay plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 5 singles, 3 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jay can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Jay has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jay's texts. The go-to partner is David Zuo (14 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U17 — we hope Yaorui Shan has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jay entered 9 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Jay's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jay and said 'nah.' Tournament committees have handed Jay a #1 or #2 seed 5 times, and Jay has failed to live up to it in 2 of them — including at 2023 U.S. Selection Event in U17 BS, where Jay was seeded #2 and finished 4th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Jay has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jay is not just losing locally — Jay is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 20% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chun family travels cross-country so Jay can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jay has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jay showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Jay traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Jay entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 23rd, which is a polite way of saying Jay paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 9 titles, 46 entries, and 37 that ended without a trophy. A player who the undisputed champion of second place in Boys' Doubles. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the moments of brilliance — Jay looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Jay. We will be watching.

jay_j_yang.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Jay J Yang, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Jay has 6 career entries across 4 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Jay saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best of 13th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jay plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 6 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

At Junior Nationals, Jay has shown up 2 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jay has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jay showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U19, which means Jay traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Jay entered 2 events with results ranging from 13th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 23rd, which is a polite way of saying Jay paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Jay keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Jay has survived 2 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Jay knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.

jayden_mar.name: Jayden Mar
jayden_mar.summary:
Out of California, Jayden Mar has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U17 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Jayden's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Jayden has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Jayden competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (11 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Jayden has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 22 different players across the career:
  • Christopher Gunawan (current primary partner) — 22 events, 1 title together in BD/U13/U15/U17, spanning 5 seasons
  • Erica Chen Lin (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)
  • Maggie Zang (current partner) — 4 events, 1 title together in U13/XD, across 2 seasons

Jayden has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jayden has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seeded entries across the career, Jayden consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 5 titles, Jayden has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
jayden_mar.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Jayden Mar, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 96 career entries in 35 tournaments, Jayden has 5 titles — a 5% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 18 entries where Jayden drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 96 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jayden saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 Dave Freeman Jr OLC in BS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jayden's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 3 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Jayden actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 14 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Now we arrive at the part of the program that Jayden's therapist probably knows by heart: the finals record. Brace yourself. Jayden has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Jayden does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Jayden has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 5 wins means Jayden is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jayden plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jayden can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Jayden has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jayden's texts. The go-to partner is Christopher Gunawan (22 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U13 — we hope Kezhen Tao has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Jayden's 5 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jayden entered 11 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jayden's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jayden and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jayden has shown up 9 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7. Tournament committees have handed Jayden a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Jayden has failed to live up to it in 2 of them — including at 2026 Bintang NC Open Local Championships in BS U17, where Jayden was seeded #1 and finished 17th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Jayden has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jayden is not just losing locally — Jayden is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Mar family travels cross-country so Jayden can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jayden has 49 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jayden showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15, which means Jayden traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 SoCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Jayden entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Jayden paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Jayden's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 5 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 91 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Jayden is a player who chokes in finals like it is a paid hobby. Will Jayden figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Jayden's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

jayden_qiu.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Jayden Qiu, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U11 level. A player who has entered 10 events across 4 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 10-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Jayden's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Jayden keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Jayden saga began at 2024 NE NJBC CRC with 3 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jayden has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jayden showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Jayden, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

jaylen_zhou.name: Jaylen Zhou
jaylen_zhou.summary:
Representing California, Jaylen Zhou embodies the relentless spirit of junior badminton, currently competing at the U15 level, with 40 career entries across 16 tournaments. In a sport that rewards persistence, Jaylen's commitment is the ultimate investment in future success.

A true triple-threat, Jaylen competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

Jaylen has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Charlie W Gao (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U15 (14 events, spanning 5 seasons)
  • Alice Luo — a trusted XD partner (8 events, spanning 3 seasons)

Jaylen has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jaylen has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Jaylen brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jaylen_zhou.roast:
Step right up and meet Jaylen Zhou, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 40 career entries in 16 tournaments, Jaylen has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 39 entries where Jaylen drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 40 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jaylen saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jaylen plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 40 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. The longest-running partnership is with Charlie W Gao: 14 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 14 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Charlie W Gao has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Alice Luo has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jaylen entered 2 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jaylen's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jaylen and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jaylen has shown up 6 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jaylen is not just losing locally — Jaylen is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhou family travels cross-country so Jaylen can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jaylen has 31 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jaylen showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13, which means Jaylen traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Jaylen entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Jaylen paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

5 seasons. 40 entries. Zero titles. If this were a stock, the SEC would have delisted it by now. If this were a restaurant, the health inspector would have questions. If this were a dating profile, the app would gently suggest 'trying a different approach.' But this is junior badminton, where hope springs eternal and so does Jaylen's entry form. We salute the commitment. We question the strategy. We will absolutely be back next season to see if anything changes. (It will not.)

jeffrey_chang.name: Jeffrey Chang
jeffrey_chang.summary:
A California competitor, Jeffrey Chang is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist, earning a podium finish representing the USA against the best young players in the Western Hemisphere (XD U19, BD U15). A top-3 finish at the Pan Am Juniors puts Jeffrey in rarified international company.

What makes Jeffrey especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 2 singles titles, 4 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior bronze medalist (XD U19, BD U15, 2019, 2024) • U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U17 BD, U17 BS) • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • 5 ORC titles.

With 7 different doubles partners over the years, Jeffrey adapts to anyone:
  • Kai Chong (current primary partner) — 8 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (8 total events in BD/U19, across 2 seasons)
  • Andrea Li (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U19/XD (6 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Chloe Ho (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #3)

The 2023-2024 season tells a story of acceleration, with 2 titles across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Jeffrey is still ascending.

Jeffrey has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jeffrey has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 10 seeded entries across the career, Jeffrey consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 6 titles, Jeffrey is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
jeffrey_chang.roast:
Clear your schedule, because we need to talk about Jeffrey Chang, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Jeffrey is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist (XD U19, BD U15), which means Jeffrey has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Jeffrey's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. But here is the thing about a bronze medal: it means Jeffrey went all the way to an international championship and STILL did not win. Jeffrey represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 43 career entries and a 14% win rate, the Pan Am bronze is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 43 entries worth of material.

The Jeffrey saga began at 2019 Pan Am Junior with 2 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2021 NorCal CRC in BD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jeffrey's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 4 titles in 12 entries — a stretch where Jeffrey actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 11 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Mixed Doubles finals and Jeffrey — name a more iconic tragedy. 2 trips to the championship match, and Jeffrey has turned 2 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Jeffrey's trophies to save time.

If Jeffrey's career were a movie, the finals record would be the part where the audience starts stress-eating popcorn. Here goes. Jeffrey has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2021 NorCal CRC, where Jeffrey entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U17, XD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Jeffrey does own 6 actual titles, so it is not like Jeffrey has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 6 wins means Jeffrey is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jeffrey plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 4 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jeffrey can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Jeffrey has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Kai Chong (8 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17 — we hope Alice Y Wang has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jeffrey entered 9 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jeffrey's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jeffrey and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jeffrey has shown up 3 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jeffrey is not just losing locally — Jeffrey is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 14% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chang family travels cross-country so Jeffrey can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jeffrey has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jeffrey showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17, which means Jeffrey traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 U.S. Selection Event in U19 BS. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Selection Event, where Jeffrey entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 15th, which is a polite way of saying Jeffrey paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Jeffrey story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 6 titles across 5 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who the undisputed champion of second place in Mixed Doubles. But every season Jeffrey comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Jeffrey, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

jennie_gai.summary:
Jennie Gai is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist — representing the United States on the international stage and coming home with gold. Winning at the Pan Am Junior Championships (Girl's Singles, Mixed Doubles, GS) is an elite, continent-level achievement. With 2 career titles across 2 tournaments, this is a player who delivers on the biggest stages.

Jennie competes in both singles and mixed doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior champion (Girl's Singles, Mixed Doubles, GS, 2017, 2021).

In doubles, Jennie has formed effective partnerships:
  • Joshua Yuan (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in Mixed

The 2021-2022 season tells a story of acceleration, with 2 titles across 1 tournaments — a clear sign that Jennie is still ascending.
jennie_gai.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Jennie Gai file just landed on our desk. Jennie hails from parts unknown. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Jennie is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist (Girl's Singles, Mixed Doubles, GS), which means Jennie has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Jennie's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. A Pan Am gold medal. International champion. The real deal. We would love to end the story right here, but unfortunately there are 3 career entries to discuss and a 67% overall win rate that suggests the Pan Am gold was the peak of a very pointy mountain with a LOT of valley on either side. Let's dig in.

The Jennie saga began at 2017 Pan Am Junior with 1 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2021 Pan Am Junior Selection in Girl's Singles, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jennie's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2021-2022 season has actually been the best yet — 2 titles in 2 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

The Jennie story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 2 titles across 2 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. But every season Jennie comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Jennie, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

jennifer_bangcaya.name: Jennifer Bangcaya
jennifer_bangcaya.summary:
Out of California, Jennifer Bangcaya is a Junior Nationals finalist — one match away from the biggest title in American junior badminton. Reaching the finals at Nationals (XD U17, GD U17, GD U15) puts Jennifer in an incredibly select group, and the experience of competing at that level is invaluable.

Notably, Jennifer has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Jennifer especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 2 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (11 entries) • Junior Nationals top-2 (XD U17, GD U17, GD U15) • 1 ORC title.

Jennifer has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 23 different players across the career:
  • Maggie Zang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U17 (10 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Vincent Chen (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U17/XD (6 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Ethan Wu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Jennifer has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jennifer has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 19 seeded entries across the career, Jennifer consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience and 3 titles, Jennifer is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
jennifer_bangcaya.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Jennifer Bangcaya, from California, continues to exist, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 96 career entries in 34 tournaments, Jennifer has 3 titles — a 3% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 31 entries where Jennifer drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 96 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jennifer saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2022 Dave Freeman OLC in XD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jennifer's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 20 entries — a stretch where Jennifer actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Jennifer is 0-for-13. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Girls' Singles finals and Jennifer — name a more iconic tragedy. 3 trips to the championship match, and Jennifer has turned 3 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Jennifer's trophies to save time. Can we talk about Jennifer and Mixed Doubles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 3 finals, 2 losses. Jennifer can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Mixed Doubles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Jennifer still cannot reach it. Can we talk about Jennifer and Girls' Doubles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 4 finals, 2 losses. Jennifer can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Girls' Doubles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Jennifer still cannot reach it.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Jennifer experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Jennifer has reached 10 championship matches across this career. That is 10 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 10 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 NW ORC, where Jennifer entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U13, GD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (7 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Jennifer does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Jennifer has never won anything. But 10 finals losses versus 3 wins means Jennifer is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jennifer plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Jennifer is 0-for-29. Zero titles in 29 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Jennifer and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Jennifer needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 23 of them, to be precise. Jennifer has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jennifer's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Maggie Zang: 10 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 10 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Maggie Zang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in GD U15 — we hope Katie Wong has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jennifer entered 11 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jennifer's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jennifer and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has been particularly cruel: 12 entries, 2 finals appearances, and zero titles. Jennifer has stood on the biggest stage in American junior badminton, one match from the most important title in the sport, and come up short 2 times. That is the kind of heartbreak that builds character — or destroys it. Time will tell.

Jennifer has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jennifer is not just losing locally — Jennifer is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Bangcaya family travels cross-country so Jennifer can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jennifer has 25 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jennifer showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U15, which means Jennifer traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Jennifer entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Jennifer paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 3 titles, 96 entries, and 93 that ended without a trophy. A player who has turned Girls' Singles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the moments of brilliance — Jennifer looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Jennifer. We will be watching.

jennifer_cui.name: Jennifer Cui
jennifer_cui.summary:
Hailing from Massachusetts, Jennifer Cui has reached the podium at Junior Nationals, finishing top-3 in GD U17. At the biggest event on the calendar, making the final four is a statement: Jennifer belongs with the best in the country.

A true triple-threat, Jennifer competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #4, 3 doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (8 entries) • Junior Nationals top-3 (GD U17) • 3 ORC titles.

With 8 different doubles partners over the years, Jennifer adapts to anyone:
  • Angie Huang (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (9 total events in GD/U19, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Siva Rajesh (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U19/XD (3 events, across 2 seasons)

Jennifer has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Jennifer has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 4 seasons and 3 titles, Jennifer has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
jennifer_cui.roast:
Allow us to introduce Jennifer Cui, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 24 career entries in 11 tournaments, Jennifer has 3 titles — a 12% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 7 entries where Jennifer drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 24 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jennifer saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2022 South Frisco ORC in GD U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jennifer's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 7 entries — a stretch where Jennifer actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Jennifer is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Every single one of Jennifer's 3 titles has come in Girls' Doubles. In U19, Girls' Singles, Mixed Doubles? Zero. Zilch. A combined 0-for-17. That is not specialization — that is a player with exactly one move, like a chess player who only knows the Queen's Gambit and panics when the opponent plays literally anything else.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jennifer plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Jennifer is 0-for-2. Zero titles in 2 tries. The best singles result is a 4th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Jennifer and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Jennifer needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Jennifer has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jennifer's texts. The most successful partnership is with Angie Huang — 9 events, 3 titles — and honestly, Angie Huang deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Angie Huang a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-25 exit at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U17 — we hope Samuel Jiaoyang Li has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jennifer entered 8 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Jennifer's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jennifer and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Jennifer 3 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jennifer is not just losing locally — Jennifer is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 12% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Cui family travels cross-country so Jennifer can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-25 finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jennifer has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jennifer showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 25th-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U17, which means Jennifer traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Jennifer entered 3 events with results ranging from 3rd to 25th. That is an average placement of 15th, which is a polite way of saying Jennifer paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Jennifer's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 3 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 21 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Jennifer is a player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. Will Jennifer figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Jennifer's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

jennifer_zhou.name: Jennifer ZHOU
jennifer_zhou.summary: Jennifer ZHOU has stepped onto the competitive stage with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Jennifer's.
jennifer_zhou.roast:
Clear your schedule, because we need to talk about Jennifer ZHOU, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Jennifer is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 ABC OLC, and so far, the only thing Jennifer has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Jennifer saga began at 2024 ABC OLC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Jennifer is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Jennifer's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Jennifer is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jennifer has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jennifer showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2024 ABC OLC in GS U13, which means Jennifer traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Jennifer is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Jennifer wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

jeremiah_wang.summary:
Out of New York, Jeremiah Wang has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U11 level, reaching the finals 2 times. With 22 entries across 10 tournaments, Jeremiah is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

A true triple-threat, Jeremiah competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Jeremiah has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Tyler Yun (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (7 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Jeremiah brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.

A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11 — we hope Elina Xiao-En Yang has emotionally recovered.

jeremy_wang.name: Jeremy Wang
jeremy_wang.summary:
Out of California, Jeremy Wang has reached the podium at Junior Nationals, finishing top-3 in BD U11. At the biggest event on the calendar, making the final four is a statement: Jeremy belongs with the best in the country.

Notably, Jeremy has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Jeremy is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals top-3 (BD U11) • ORC top-2 finisher.

With 9 different doubles partners over the years, Jeremy adapts to anyone:
  • Leslie Wang (current primary partner) — 9 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (14 total events in BD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Joanna Deng (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Aayush Jaladurgam (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #3)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 5 podium finishes across 5 tournaments. Jeremy is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Jeremy has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Jeremy has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Jeremy brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jeremy_wang.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Jeremy Wang, from California, continues to exist, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-3. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Jeremy has 50 career entries across 17 tournaments and has managed to win 3 of them — a 6% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Jeremy saga began at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC), where Jeremy burst onto the scene with a title in BS U11 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Jeremy has delivered on some of those promises: 3 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Jeremy promised and what Jeremy has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in.

In Boys' Doubles, Jeremy has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 4 championship appearances, 3 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Jeremy chokes in Boys' Doubles finals specifically.

If Jeremy's career were a movie, the finals record would be the part where the audience starts stress-eating popcorn. Here goes. Jeremy has reached 6 championship matches across this career. That is 6 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 6 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 Dave Freeman Jr OLC, where Jeremy entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U11, BD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (5 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Jeremy does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Jeremy has never won anything. But 6 finals losses versus 3 wins means Jeremy is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jeremy plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jeremy can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Jeremy has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jeremy's texts. The go-to partner is Leslie Wang (14 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in XD U13 — we hope Joanna Deng has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Jeremy's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. Junior Nationals has seen Jeremy 3 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

Jeremy has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jeremy is not just losing locally — Jeremy is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wang family travels cross-country so Jeremy can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jeremy has 18 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jeremy showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U11, which means Jeremy traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Jeremy entered 3 events with results ranging from 4th to 65th. That is an average placement of 28th, which is a polite way of saying Jeremy paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Jeremy story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 3 titles across 3 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who the undisputed champion of second place in Boys' Doubles. But every season Jeremy comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Jeremy, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.
