jerry_yuan.name: Jerry Yuan
jerry_yuan.summary:
Out of California, Jerry Yuan is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist — representing the United States on the international stage and coming home with gold. Winning at the Pan Am Junior Championships (XD U15, BD U15) is an elite, continent-level achievement. With 3 career titles across 11 tournaments, this is a player who delivers on the biggest stages.

Jerry is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 3 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior champion (XD U15, BD U15, 2019) • U.S. Selection Event top-3 (U19 BD) • Junior Nationals champion (BD U17) • ORC top-3 finisher.

With 6 different doubles partners over the years, Jerry adapts to anyone:
  • Hailey Hung (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U19/XD (8 events, best: #3, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Adam Tay (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U19 (5 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Arthur Heng Lee — 5 events, 1 title together in BD/U19, across 2 seasons
  • Arthur Lee — 2 events in BD, producing 2 titles together

Jerry has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning NorCal, SoCal, South, Jerry isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Over 5 seasons and 3 titles, Jerry has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
jerry_yuan.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Jerry Yuan, from California, continues to exist, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Jerry is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist (XD U15, BD U15), which means Jerry has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Jerry's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. A Pan Am gold medal. International champion. The real deal. We would love to end the story right here, but unfortunately there are 25 career entries to discuss and a 12% overall win rate that suggests the Pan Am gold was the peak of a very pointy mountain with a LOT of valley on either side. Let's dig in.

The Jerry saga began at 2019 Pan Am Junior, where Jerry burst onto the scene with a title in BD U15 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Jerry has delivered on some of those promises: 3 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Jerry promised and what Jerry has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2018-2019 season — 2 titles in 4 entries — a stretch where Jerry actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Jerry is 0-for-2. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Take away Boys' Doubles and Jerry has never won anything. Not once. 16 entries across other disciplines, zero titles. Jerry is essentially a one-club golfer who showed up to a tournament with 18 holes and one iron.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jerry plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Jerry is 0-for-1. Zero titles in 1 tries. The best singles result is a 17th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Jerry and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Jerry needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Jerry has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Hailey Hung: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Hailey Hung has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U19 — we hope Arthur Heng Lee has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jerry entered 6 events with results ranging from top-3 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Jerry's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jerry and said 'nah.'

Jerry has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jerry has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jerry showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, which means Jerry traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Jerry entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 17th. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Jerry paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Jerry story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 3 titles across 5 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. But every season Jerry comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Jerry, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

jessica_wu.name: Jessica Wu
jessica_wu.summary:
Out of California, Jessica Wu is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-2 finish at Selection places Jessica in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Notably, Jessica has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Jessica is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #4, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U15 XD, U15 GD) • Junior Nationals champion (XD U17) • 1 ORC title.

Jessica has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 9 different players across the career:
  • Ansen Liu (current primary partner) — 7 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (7 total events in U19/XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Chang He (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (16 total events in GD/U15/U19, spanning 5 seasons)

Having moved up to U19, Jessica is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Jessica brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Jessica has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jessica has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 7 seeded entries across the career, Jessica consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 2 titles, Jessica has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
jessica_wu.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Jessica Wu, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-2. And yes, Jessica IS a Junior National Champion — XD U17 — which is a legitimate, no-asterisks, put-it-on-the-college-application achievement. We will give that its due respect for about five seconds... okay, time's up. Because behind those headlines is a 64-entry career across 22 tournaments with a 3% win rate, which means 62 times — 62! — Jessica went home without a trophy. So buckle in, because we have 64 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jessica saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 7th. The first title would not come until 2024 Frisco South ORC in GD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jessica's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 2 titles in 12 entries — a stretch where Jessica actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Jessica is 0-for-14. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

In Girls' Doubles, Jessica has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 2 championship appearances, 1 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Jessica chokes in Girls' Doubles finals specifically. Mixed Doubles finals and Jessica — name a more iconic tragedy. 2 trips to the championship match, and Jessica has turned 1 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Jessica's trophies to save time.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Jessica experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Jessica has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Jessica does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Jessica has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 2 wins means Jessica is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jessica plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Jessica is 0-for-18. Zero titles in 18 tries. The best singles result is a 4th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Jessica and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Jessica needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Jessica has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jessica's texts. The go-to partner is Chang He (16 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U17 — we hope Caden Wang has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jessica entered 12 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Jessica's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jessica and said 'nah.'

Jessica has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jessica is not just losing locally — Jessica is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wu family travels cross-country so Jessica can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jessica has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jessica showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U17, which means Jessica traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Jessica entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Jessica paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Jessica's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 2 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 62 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Jessica is a player who has turned Girls' Doubles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning. Will Jessica figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Jessica's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

jessica_zhang.name: Jessica Zhang
jessica_zhang.summary:

Jessica is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Jessica especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 5 singles titles, 4 doubles titles, 4 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (8 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

The numbers tell a compelling story: Jessica boasts a career +790 point differential, a commanding 71% win rate across 82 matches, 49 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively.

With 25 different doubles partners over the years, Jessica adapts to anyone:
  • Kingsley Chong (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #5)
  • Zi Yang (current partner) — 5 events, 1 title together in GD, spanning 4 seasons
  • Megha Challa (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in GD, across 2 seasons
  • Bernice Xu (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #5)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Jessica has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jessica has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 17 seeded entries across the career, Jessica consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 13 titles, Jessica has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
jessica_zhang.roast:
Allow us to introduce Jessica Zhang, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 76 career entries in 27 tournaments, Jessica has 13 titles — a 17% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 4 entries where Jessica drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 76 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jessica saga began at 2022 NE CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2023 MassBad NE OLC in GS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jessica's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 6 titles in 14 entries — a stretch where Jessica actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 16 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

GS U15 used to be Jessica's personal ATM — just show up and collect the gold. That was the 2024 NE MassBad OLC era. Now Jessica is grinding for a top-2 finish in the same event. The shuttle giveth, and the shuttle taketh away. In XD U15, Jessica was once THE player to beat — 2 titles including at 2025 NE MassBad OLC. Fast forward to now, Jessica is finishing 5th, which is the competitive equivalent of an ex showing up to your wedding with a better-looking date. GD U15 used to be Jessica's personal ATM — just show up and collect the gold. That was the 2024 NE MassBad OLC era. Now Jessica is grinding for a top-5 finish in the same event. The shuttle giveth, and the shuttle taketh away. And that is just 3 of the 5 events where Jessica's reign has ended. The throne room is getting emptier by the tournament.

Here is a fun detail: in GS U15, Jessica used to be seeded #1 (at 2024 NE MassBad OLC). The most recent seeding? #2 (at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC). Tournament committees are literally telling Jessica 'you are getting worse at this' — in writing, on the official draw sheet, for everyone to see. That has to sting.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jessica plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 5 singles, 4 doubles, 4 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jessica can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 25 of them, to be precise. Jessica has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jessica's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Emily Min Tang: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Emily Min Tang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Tao Peng has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Jessica's 13 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jessica entered 2 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-5. The Selection committee looked at Jessica's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jessica and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jessica has shown up 8 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5. Tournament committees have handed Jessica a #1 or #2 seed 4 times, and Jessica has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U15, where Jessica was seeded #2 and finished 3rd. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

One thing nobody can accuse Jessica of is playing it safe. Jessica has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Jessica has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 7 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Jessica has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jessica is not just losing locally — Jessica is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 17% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Jessica can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jessica has 23 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jessica showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15, which means Jessica traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Jessica entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Jessica paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Jessica story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 13 titles across 5 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who proof that glory has an expiration date and Jessica's is past due. But every season Jessica comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Jessica, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

A player who has entered 11 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 11-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Jessie's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Jessie keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Jessie saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 11 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Jessie has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jessie has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jessie showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 SoCal ORC in BD U15 — we hope Kezhen Tao has emotionally recovered.

Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wen family travels cross-country so Jianxi can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Jianxi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Jianxi is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Jianxi the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Jianxi. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

Not a trophy, obviously — Jiaxuan knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.

jiayu_amy_chen.name: Jiayu Amy Chen
jiayu_amy_chen.summary:
Out of New York, Jiayu Amy Chen is in the early chapters of a competitive journey, currently competing at the U19 level, with 5 entries across 2 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

Notably, Jiayu has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Jiayu is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Jiayu has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
jiayu_amy_chen.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Jiayu Amy Chen, from New York, continues to exist, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 5 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Jiayu is right on schedule. It all started at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19), and so far, the only thing Jiayu has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Jiayu saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jiayu plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 5 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Jiayu has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jiayu has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jiayu showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U17/U19) in GS U17, which means Jiayu traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 NE ORC (U17/U19), where Jiayu entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Jiayu paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Jiayu keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Jiayu has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Jiayu, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

jida_wu.name: Jida Wu
jida_wu.summary:
A Texas competitor, Jida Wu has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Jida's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Jida is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Jida especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

The statistics paint a vivid picture — Jida boasts a biggest win margin of 37 points, 49 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a solid 61% win rate over 97 matches.

Jida has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 20 different players across the career:
  • Liam Xiao (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (7 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Mrugakshi Kulkarni (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Sienna Lee (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Harrison Zhang (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 5 podium finishes across 7 tournaments. Jida is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Jida has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jida has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Jida brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jida_wu.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Jida Wu file just landed on our desk. Jida hails from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Jida has 60 career entries across 21 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 3% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Jida saga began at 2023 South OLC (TBTT) with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC in BD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jida's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Jida would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times Jida has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times Jida has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Jida's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jida plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jida can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Jida has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jida's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Liam Xiao: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Liam Xiao has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Miura Yan Faye Goh has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Jida's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Jida has shown up 6 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

One thing nobody can accuse Jida of is playing it safe. Jida has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15 — which means Jida has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 2 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Jida has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jida is not just losing locally — Jida is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wu family travels cross-country so Jida can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jida has 28 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jida showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13, which means Jida traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Jida entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Jida paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Jida story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 2 titles across 3 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases. But every season Jida comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Jida, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

Season after season, Jiyul keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Jiyul has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Jiyul, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

joanna_deng.name: Joanna Deng
joanna_deng.summary:
Representing California, Joanna Deng broke through with a tournament title, currently competing at the U15 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 63 entries across 22 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Joanna is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

Joanna has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 21 different players across the career:
  • Crystal Yao (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (5 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Jeremy Wang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Sophia Coco Wang (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Bliss Liu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Joanna has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Joanna has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 4 seasons and 1 title, Joanna has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
joanna_deng.roast:
Step right up and meet Joanna Deng, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 63 career entries in 22 tournaments, Joanna has 1 title — a 2% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 62 entries where Joanna drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 63 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Joanna saga began at 2022 SoCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 OBA NW OLC in GD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Joanna's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 24 entries — a stretch where Joanna actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Joanna is 0-for-11. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Remember when Joanna used to win GD U13? Because Joanna sure does — probably replays it in slow motion every night before bed. Joanna took gold at 2024 OBA NW OLC, and since then? Best finish: 9th. The crown did not just slip — it packed its bags and left.

In Mixed Doubles, Joanna has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 3 championship appearances, 3 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Joanna chokes in Mixed Doubles finals specifically.

Now we arrive at the part of the program that Joanna's therapist probably knows by heart: the finals record. Brace yourself. Joanna has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Joanna does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Joanna has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 1 wins means Joanna is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Joanna plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Joanna is 0-for-21. Zero titles in 21 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Joanna and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Joanna needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 21 of them, to be precise. Joanna has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Joanna's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Evan Lu: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Evan Lu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Zachary Wang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Joanna's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Joanna entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Joanna's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Joanna and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Joanna has shown up 6 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Joanna is not just losing locally — Joanna is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Deng family travels cross-country so Joanna can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Joanna has 32 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Joanna showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U13, which means Joanna traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Joanna entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Joanna paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Joanna's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 1 title shine bright, but they are surrounded by 62 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Joanna is a player who former champion currently in witness protection from their own legacy. Will Joanna figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Joanna's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

joannah_stephen.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Joannah Stephen, from Oregon, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 11 events across 7 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 11-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Joannah's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Joannah keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Joannah saga began at 2023 NW OLC with 2 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Joannah has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Joannah showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in GS U15, which means Joannah traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Bellevue NW ORC, where Joannah entered 2 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Joannah paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 4 seasons and 11 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Joannah is not most humans. Joannah is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Joannah the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Joannah. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

Joanne competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Joanne has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. joanne_li.name: Joanne Li
joanne_li.summary:
Representing North Carolina, Joanne Li is a Pan American Junior Championships silver medalist, earning a podium finish representing the USA against the best young players in the Western Hemisphere (GD U15, XD U17, GS U15). A top-2 finish at the Pan Am Juniors puts Joanne in rarified international company.

Joanne is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (6 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Joanne is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 8 singles titles, 8 doubles titles, 8 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior silver medalist (GD U15, XD U17, GS U15, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event champion (U13 XD, U15 GS, U13 GS, U13 GD, U17 XD, U15 GD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (GD U13, GS U13, GS U11, GS U15, XD U11) • 13 ORC titles.

With 15 different doubles partners over the years, Joanne adapts to anyone:
  • Kimaya Krishna (current primary partner) — 12 events together this season alone with 5 titles, building serious momentum (19 total events in GD/U13/U15, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Rithik Rasamsetti (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (9 total events in U17/XD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Ethan Wu — 2 events, 1 title together in XD
  • Terence Cai — 8 events, 1 title together in U13/XD, spanning 3 seasons

The 2024-2025 season has been outstanding, with 15 podium finishes across 6 tournaments. Joanne is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Joanne has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Joanne has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Joanne has been seeded #1 or #2 in 10 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 5 seasons and 29 titles, Joanne has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
joanne_li.roast:
Step right up and meet Joanne Li, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U17 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Joanne is a Pan American Junior Championships silver medalist (GD U15, XD U17, GS U15), which means Joanne has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Joanne's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. But here is the thing about a silver medal: it means Joanne went all the way to an international championship and STILL did not win. Joanne represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 77 career entries and a 38% win rate, the Pan Am silver is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 77 entries worth of material.

The Joanne saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Joanne burst onto the scene with a title in GS U11 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Joanne has delivered on some of those promises: 29 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Joanne promised and what Joanne has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 11 titles in 19 entries — a stretch where Joanne actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 5 wins in 19 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

In GS U15, Joanne was once THE player to beat — 3 titles including at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC. These days, Joanne is finishing 3rd, which is the competitive equivalent of an ex showing up to your wedding with a better-looking date. In GD U15, Joanne was once THE player to beat — 3 titles including at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. These days, Joanne is finishing 2nd, which is the competitive equivalent of an ex showing up to your wedding with a better-looking date. In GS U17, Joanne was once THE player to beat — 1 title including at 2023 NE OLC (NVBC). Fast forward to now, Joanne is finishing 7th, which is the competitive equivalent of an ex showing up to your wedding with a better-looking date. And that is just 3 of the 5 events where Joanne's reign has ended. The throne room is getting emptier by the tournament.

The seeding trajectory in GS U17 tells a story: #2 at 2023 South OLC (TBTT), down to #4 at 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19). When the people whose job is to rank you start ranking you lower, that is not a slump — that is a trend line with a very clear direction, and it is not pointing up.

If Joanne's career were a movie, the finals record would be the part where the audience starts stress-eating popcorn. Here goes. Joanne has reached 16 championship matches across this career. That is 16 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 16 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19), where Joanne entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U17, GD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (10 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Joanne does own 29 actual titles, so it is not like Joanne has never won anything. But 16 finals losses versus 29 wins means Joanne is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Joanne plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 8 singles, 8 doubles, 8 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Joanne can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Joanne has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Joanne's texts. The most successful partnership is with Kimaya Krishna — 19 events, 10 titles — and honestly, Kimaya Krishna deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Kimaya Krishna a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Yifan Liu has emotionally recovered.

Then there is the seeding situation. Joanne has been seeded in 19 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Joanne takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2023 South CRC in GS U15, and finishing 9th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Joanne. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Joanne of is playing it safe. Joanne has entered older age groups at 6 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17 — which means Joanne has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 27 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Joanne has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Joanne is not just losing locally — Joanne is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 38% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Li family travels cross-country so Joanne can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Joanne has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Joanne showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U15, which means Joanne traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-25 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Joanne entered 3 events with results ranging from 4th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Joanne paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Joanne's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 29 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 48 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Joanne is a player who proof that glory has an expiration date and Joanne's is past due. Will Joanne figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Joanne's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

  • Jaden Zhou — a trusted U15/XD partner (6 events, best: #5, spanning 3 seasons)

jocelyn_youran_zhang.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Jocelyn (Youran) Zhang, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 88 career entries in 31 tournaments, Jocelyn has 2 titles — a 2% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 43 entries where Jocelyn drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 88 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jocelyn saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2023 NW OLC in GD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jocelyn's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 2 titles in 26 entries — a stretch where Jocelyn actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Jocelyn is 0-for-8. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Remember when Jocelyn used to win GS U17? Because Jocelyn sure does — probably replays it in slow motion every night before bed. Jocelyn took gold at 2023 NW OLC, and since then? Best finish: 2nd. The crown did not just slip — it packed its bags and left.

In Girls' Singles, Jocelyn has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 2 championship appearances, 1 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Jocelyn chokes in Girls' Singles finals specifically.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jocelyn plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jocelyn can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Jocelyn has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jocelyn's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Zimeng Zhou: 16 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 16 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Zimeng Zhou has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Ethan Biren Fong has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Jocelyn's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jocelyn entered 9 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jocelyn's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jocelyn and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jocelyn has shown up 9 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

One thing nobody can accuse Jocelyn of is playing it safe. Jocelyn has entered older age groups at 9 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Jocelyn has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 2 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Jocelyn has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jocelyn is not just losing locally — Jocelyn is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Jocelyn can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jocelyn has 43 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jocelyn showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in XD U15, which means Jocelyn traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Jocelyn entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Jocelyn paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Jocelyn's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 2 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 86 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Jocelyn is a player who former champion currently in witness protection from their own legacy. Will Jocelyn figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Jocelyn's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

jocelyn_chiang.roast:
Clear your schedule, because we need to talk about Jocelyn Chiang, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Jocelyn has 53 career entries across 18 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 4% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Jocelyn saga began at 2022 NorCal ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2026 Dave Freeman Jr. SoCal OLC in XD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jocelyn's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 2 titles in 18 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

In Girls' Doubles, Jocelyn has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 2 championship appearances, 1 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Jocelyn chokes in Girls' Doubles finals specifically.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jocelyn plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Jocelyn is 0-for-18. Zero titles in 18 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Jocelyn and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Jocelyn needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Jocelyn has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jocelyn's texts. The go-to partner is Andy Korlann (4 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Ryan Chu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Jocelyn's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Jocelyn has shown up 3 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jocelyn is not just losing locally — Jocelyn is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chiang family travels cross-country so Jocelyn can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jocelyn has 30 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jocelyn showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U13, which means Jocelyn traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Jocelyn entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Jocelyn paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 2 titles, 53 entries, and 51 that ended without a trophy. A player who the undisputed champion of second place in Girls' Doubles. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the moments of brilliance — Jocelyn looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Jocelyn. We will be watching.
