What makes Joey especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 4 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior bronze medalist (BD U15, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U15 XD, U15 BD) • Junior Nationals champion (BD U15) • 2 ORC titles.

The numbers tell a compelling story: Joey boasts a career +618 point differential, a biggest win margin of 30 points, a commanding 76% win rate across 76 matches.

Joey has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 22 different players across the career:
  • William Geng (current primary partner) — 13 events together this season alone with 3 titles, building serious momentum (13 total events in BD/U15, across 2 seasons)
  • Sophia Z Liu (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD, across 2 seasons

Having moved up to U19, Joey is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Joey brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

Joey has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Joey has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 4 seasons and 5 titles, Joey has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
joey_liu.roast:
Allow us to introduce Joey Liu, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Joey is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist (BD U15), which means Joey has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Joey's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. But here is the thing about a bronze medal: it means Joey went all the way to an international championship and STILL did not win. Joey represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 74 career entries and a 7% win rate, the Pan Am bronze is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 74 entries worth of material.

The Joey saga began at 2022 NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 33rd. The first title would not come until 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Joey's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 5 titles in 29 entries — a stretch where Joey actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Joey is 0-for-13. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Joey reaches a final. Joey has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Joey does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Joey has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 5 wins means Joey is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Joey plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Joey is 0-for-24. Zero titles in 24 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Joey and suddenly the wins start appearing — 5 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Joey needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Joey has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Joey's texts. The most successful partnership is with William Geng — 13 events, 3 titles — and honestly, William Geng deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send William Geng a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Rex Hong has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Joey entered 3 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Joey's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Joey and said 'nah.'

One thing nobody can accuse Joey of is playing it safe. Joey has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Joey has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 5 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Joey has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Joey is not just losing locally — Joey is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Liu family travels cross-country so Joey can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Joey has 29 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Joey showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Joey traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Joey entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Joey paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 5 titles, 74 entries, and 69 that ended without a trophy. A player who has a finals record that should come with a trigger warning. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the moments of brilliance — Joey looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Joey. We will be watching.

joey_sun.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Joey Sun, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 14 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 14-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Joey's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Joey keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Joey saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Joey plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 14 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Joey has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Joey has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Joey showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Joey traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Joey entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Joey paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Joey keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Joey has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Joey knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.

joey_wang.name: Joey Wang
joey_wang.summary:
A California competitor, Joey Wang stands as an ORC champion, currently competing at the U15 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

Joey is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Joey competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 3 singles titles, 3 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • 2 ORC titles.

Diving into the match data reveals: Joey boasts a biggest win margin of 32 points, a commanding 73% win rate across 90 matches, a career +679 point differential.

Joey has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 23 different players across the career:
  • Arnav Satish (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U13 (7 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Daisy Xu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #2)
  • Trisha Mehta (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD, across 2 seasons
  • Eddie Sun (current partner) — 3 events, 1 title together in BD, across 2 seasons

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 4 titles across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Joey is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Joey has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Joey has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 8 seeded entries across the career, Joey consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 8 titles, Joey is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
joey_wang.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Joey Wang file just landed on our desk. Joey hails from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 73 career entries in 25 tournaments, Joey has 8 titles — a 11% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 8 entries where Joey drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 73 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Joey saga began at 2022 SoCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) in BD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Joey's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 4 titles in 18 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Mixed Doubles finals and Joey — name a more iconic tragedy. 3 trips to the championship match, and Joey has turned 1 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Joey's trophies to save time.

Now we arrive at the part of the program that Joey's therapist probably knows by heart: the finals record. Brace yourself. Joey has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Joey does own 8 actual titles, so it is not like Joey has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 8 wins means Joey is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Joey plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 3 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Joey can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 23 of them, to be precise. Joey has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Joey's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Arnav Satish: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Arnav Satish has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U13 — we hope Ryan Li has emotionally recovered.

Of Joey's 8 career titles, 6 came at local events versus 2 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Joey feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Joey entered 3 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Joey's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Joey and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Joey has shown up 9 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

One thing nobody can accuse Joey of is playing it safe. Joey has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15 — which means Joey has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 7 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Joey has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Joey is not just losing locally — Joey is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 11% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wang family travels cross-country so Joey can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Joey has 32 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Joey showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Joey traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Joey entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Joey paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 8 titles, 73 entries, and 65 that ended without a trophy. A player who has turned Mixed Doubles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the moments of brilliance — Joey looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Joey. We will be watching.

jogesh_nihanth_kadikatla.name: Jogesh Nihanth Kadikatla
jogesh_nihanth_kadikatla.summary:
Representing North Carolina, Jogesh Nihanth Kadikatla is a multi-time champion on the junior circuit, currently competing at the U17 level, capturing 2 titles across 10 tournaments and 24 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Jogesh keeps finding ways to come out on top.

What makes Jogesh especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (4 entries).

Jogesh has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 6 different players across the career:
  • Nathan Kankanala (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (9 total events in BD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Jennifer Xu — 1 events, 1 title together in XD

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Jogesh isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Jogesh brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jogesh_nihanth_kadikatla.roast:
Step right up and meet Jogesh Nihanth Kadikatla, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Jogesh has 24 career entries across 10 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 8% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Jogesh saga began at 2023 South ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 33rd. The first title would not come until 2024 NE MassBad OLC in XD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jogesh's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 2 titles in 12 entries — a stretch where Jogesh actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Jogesh is 0-for-2. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jogesh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Jogesh is 0-for-10. Zero titles in 10 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Jogesh and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Jogesh needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Jogesh has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Nathan Kankanala (9 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U17 — we hope Eddy Li has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Jogesh's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Jogesh has shown up 4 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Jogesh has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jogesh has 16 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jogesh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, which means Jogesh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Jogesh entered 2 events with results ranging from 25th to 65th. That is an average placement of 45th, which is a polite way of saying Jogesh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Jogesh's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 2 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 22 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Jogesh is a player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. Will Jogesh figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Jogesh's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

john_junhui_shi.name: John Junhui Shi
john_junhui_shi.summary:
Hailing from New York, John Junhui Shi is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U15 level, with 3 entries across 2 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

John competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
john_junhui_shi.roast:

johnathan_h_chi.roast:
Allow us to introduce Johnathan H Chi, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 15 career entries in 6 tournaments, Johnathan has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 14 entries where Johnathan drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 15 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Johnathan saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Johnathan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 15 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Johnathan has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Ashley Zhang: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Ashley Zhang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in XD U19 — we hope Ashley Zhang has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Johnathan entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Johnathan's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Johnathan and said 'nah.'

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Johnathan is not just losing locally — Johnathan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chi family travels cross-country so Johnathan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-17 finish at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Johnathan has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Johnathan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in BS U19, which means Johnathan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip.

With only 15 entries on the books, Johnathan is basically a rookie — which is the nicest possible way of saying 'there is not enough data to roast properly yet.' But give it time. The circuit is long, the shuttles are expensive, and we are patient. Come back in a season, Johnathan. We will have more material. We always do.

  • Cayden Wang — 2 events, 1 title together in BD

Johnny has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

johnny_chen.roast:
Allow us to introduce Johnny Chen, from Washington, currently competing at the U13 level. An Open Regional Championship titlist with 1 ORC title, which sounds very official — and it is, ORCs are the real deal. But let's zoom out: that's 1 wins out of 13 ORC entries, and an overall career of 2-for-16 (12%). Regional royalty, national afterthought. A big deal on the local circuit, a footnote everywhere else. Let's dig in.

The Johnny saga began at 2021 NW CRC, where Johnny burst onto the scene with a title in BS U11 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Johnny has delivered on some of those promises: 2 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Johnny promised and what Johnny has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 13 entries — a stretch where Johnny actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Johnny is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Johnny plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Johnny can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Johnny has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Johnny's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BD U13 — we hope Cayden Wang has emotionally recovered.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Johnny is not just losing locally — Johnny is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 12% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chen family travels cross-country so Johnny can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Johnny has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Johnny showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Johnny traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 NW ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Johnny entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Johnny paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 2 titles, 16 entries, and 14 that ended without a trophy. A player who changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the moments of brilliance — Johnny looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Johnny. We will be watching.

jolie_wang.name: Jolie Wang
jolie_wang.summary:
Jolie Wang is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist — representing the United States on the international stage and coming home with gold. Winning at the Pan Am Junior Championships (GD U15, XD U19, GS U15) is an elite, continent-level achievement. With 2 career titles across 6 tournaments, this is a player who delivers on the biggest stages.

What makes Jolie especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 2 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior champion (GD U15, XD U19, GS U15, 2017, 2022) • U.S. Selection Event top-3 (U19 XD) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Jolie has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 5 different players across the career:
  • Andrew Yuan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U19/XD (4 events, best: #3)
  • Edward Zhang (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #4)
  • Emily Han (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U19 (2 events)

The 2021-2022 season has been outstanding, with 5 podium finishes across 5 tournaments. Jolie is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Jolie has competed at both U15 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Jolie's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
jolie_wang.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Jolie Wang, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Jolie is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist (GD U15, XD U19, GS U15), which means Jolie has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Jolie's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. A Pan Am gold medal. International champion. The real deal. We would love to end the story right here, but unfortunately there are 12 career entries to discuss and a 17% overall win rate that suggests the Pan Am gold was the peak of a very pointy mountain with a LOT of valley on either side. Let's dig in.

The Jolie saga began at 2017 Pan Am Junior, where Jolie burst onto the scene with a title in GD U15 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Jolie has delivered on some of those promises: 2 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Jolie promised and what Jolie has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2016-2017 season — 2 titles in 3 entries — a stretch where Jolie actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Jolie is 0-for-9. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Jolie would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Jolie has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Jolie has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Jolie's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jolie plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Jolie is 0-for-1. Zero titles in 1 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Jolie and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Jolie needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Jolie has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Andrew Yuan (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jolie entered 2 events with results ranging from top-3 to a humbling round-of-9. The Selection committee looked at Jolie's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jolie and said 'nah.'

Jolie has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

In the end, Jolie's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 2 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 10 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Jolie is a player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. Will Jolie figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Jolie's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

Joline represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 35 career entries and a 11% win rate, the Pan Am bronze is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 35 entries worth of material.

The Joline saga began at 2019 Pan Am Junior with 2 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in GD U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Joline's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 3 titles in 15 entries — a stretch where Joline actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Joline is 0-for-3. Girls' Doubles finals and Joline — name a more iconic tragedy. 4 trips to the championship match, and Joline has turned 3 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Joline's trophies to save time.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Joline reaches a final. Joline has reached 9 championship matches across this career. That is 9 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 9 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Joline entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U19, GD U19). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (5 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Joline does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Joline has never won anything. But 9 finals losses versus 4 wins means Joline is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Joline plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Joline can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Joline has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Joline's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Robert Shekhtman: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Robert Shekhtman has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U19 — we hope Robert Shekhtman has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Joline entered 9 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-5. The Selection committee looked at Joline's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Joline and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Joline has competed 3 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one. Tournament committees have handed Joline a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Joline has failed to live up to it in 2 of them — including at 2023 U.S. Selection Event in U19 GS, where Joline was seeded #2 and finished 5th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Joline has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Joline is not just losing locally — Joline is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 11% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Siu family travels cross-country so Joline can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-49 finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Joline has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Joline showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U17, which means Joline traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Joline entered 3 events with results ranging from 2nd to 49th. That is an average placement of 20th, which is a polite way of saying Joline paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Joline story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 4 titles across 5 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who has turned Girls' Doubles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning. But every season Joline comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Joline, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

jonah_chenfei_wong.roast:
Step right up and meet Jonah Chenfei Wong, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 54 career entries in 19 tournaments, Jonah has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 53 entries where Jonah drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 54 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jonah saga began at 2022 NorCal ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jonah plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 54 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Jonah has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jonah's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Alan Ma: 15 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 15 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Alan Ma has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Netri Vasireddy has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jonah entered 2 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jonah's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jonah and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jonah has shown up 9 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jonah is not just losing locally — Jonah is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wong family travels cross-country so Jonah can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jonah has 46 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jonah showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13, which means Jonah traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Jonah entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Jonah paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 4 seasons and 54 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Jonah is not most humans. Jonah is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Jonah the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Jonah. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

Someone get the popcorn, because the Jonathan Cai file just landed on our desk. Jonathan hails from New York, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Jonathan has 34 career entries across 14 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Jonathan saga began at 2022 NE CRC with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jonathan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 34 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Jonathan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jonathan's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Wayne Chien: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Wayne Chien has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BD U13 — we hope Wayne Chien has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Jonathan has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Jonathan has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jonathan has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jonathan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13, which means Jonathan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15), where Jonathan entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. So here we are: 4 seasons, 34 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Jonathan just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Jonathan has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Jonathan. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Jonathan: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

jonathan_liu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jonathan Liu, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Jonathan has 17 career entries across 8 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Jonathan saga began at 2021 NW CRC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jonathan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 17 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Jonathan has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Leonard Wang (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U17 — we hope Jiongchen Gu has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Jonathan has shown up 4 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Jonathan has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jonathan has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jonathan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Jonathan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Jonathan entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Jonathan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Jonathan keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Jonathan has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Jonathan knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.

jonathan_loh.name: Jonathan Loh
jonathan_loh.summary:
A California competitor, Jonathan Loh has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U15 level, breaking into the semifinals 2 times. Across 58 entries in 21 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Notably, Jonathan has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Jonathan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries).

Jonathan has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 11 different players across the career:
  • Ethan Xiao-ze Yang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U13 (17 events, best: #5, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Sherlyn Mei (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Jonathan has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jonathan has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Jonathan brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jonathan_loh.roast:
Clear your schedule, because we need to talk about Jonathan Loh, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 58 career entries in 21 tournaments, Jonathan has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 57 entries where Jonathan drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 58 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jonathan saga began at 2022 NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jonathan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 58 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Jonathan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jonathan's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Ethan Xiao-ze Yang: 17 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 17 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Ethan Xiao-ze Yang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U13 — we hope Ethan Xiao-ze Yang has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jonathan entered 2 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jonathan's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jonathan and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jonathan has shown up 9 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Jonathan has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jonathan is not just losing locally — Jonathan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Loh family travels cross-country so Jonathan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jonathan has 32 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jonathan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Jonathan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Jonathan entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Jonathan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 4 seasons and 58 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Jonathan is not most humans. Jonathan is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Jonathan the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Jonathan. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jonathan's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Prisha Rastogi has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Jonathan has shown up 3 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13, which means Jonathan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Jonathan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Jonathan keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Jonathan has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Jonathan knows that better than anyone — but something. Also, Jonathan changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases, which we felt needed mentioning one more time.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Jonathan brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.

Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Jonathan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

jonathan_wu.name: Jonathan Wu
jonathan_wu.summary:
A New Jersey competitor, Jonathan Wu is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-4 finish at Selection places Jonathan in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

A true triple-threat, Jonathan competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, 3 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U13 BD) • Junior Nationals competitor (11 entries) • 2 ORC titles.

Jonathan has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 22 different players across the career:
  • Luke Han (current primary partner) — 18 events, 1 title together in BD/U13/U15, spanning 5 seasons
  • Ruohan Joy Du (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)
  • Annabelle Claire Cheng (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Daniel Su (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in BD, across 2 seasons

Jonathan has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jonathan has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Jonathan has been seeded #1 or #2 in 7 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 6 seasons and 5 titles, Jonathan has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
jonathan_wu.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Jonathan Wu file just landed on our desk. Jonathan hails from New Jersey, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-4. Top-4 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 95 career entries in 33 tournaments, Jonathan has 5 titles — a 5% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 18 entries where Jonathan drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 95 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jonathan saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 6th. The first title would not come until 2021 NE ORC (U11-U13) in XD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jonathan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 3 titles in 23 entries — a stretch where Jonathan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Jonathan is 0-for-9. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Remember when Jonathan used to win XD U15? Because Jonathan sure does — probably replays it in slow motion every night before bed. Jonathan took gold at 2024 NE CRC (NJBC), and since then? Best finish: 9th. The crown did not just slip — it packed its bags and left. BD U15 used to be Jonathan's personal ATM — just show up and collect the gold. That was the 2024 NE OLC (NVBC) era. Now Jonathan is grinding for a top-17 finish in the same event. The shuttle giveth, and the shuttle taketh away.

Here is a fun detail: in BS U15, Jonathan used to be seeded #1 (at 2023 MassBad NE OLC). The most recent seeding? #8 (at 2025 Capital NE OLC). Tournament committees are literally telling Jonathan 'you are getting worse at this' — in writing, on the official draw sheet, for everyone to see. That has to sting.

If Jonathan's career were a movie, the finals record would be the part where the audience starts stress-eating popcorn. Here goes. Jonathan has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Jonathan does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Jonathan has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 5 wins means Jonathan is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jonathan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Jonathan is 0-for-30. Zero titles in 30 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Jonathan and suddenly the wins start appearing — 5 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Jonathan needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Jonathan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jonathan's texts. The go-to partner is Luke Han (18 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Irene Yan has emotionally recovered.

Of Jonathan's 5 career titles, 3 came at local events versus 2 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Jonathan feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jonathan entered 9 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jonathan's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jonathan and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jonathan has shown up 11 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6. Then there is the seeding situation. Jonathan has been seeded in 14 events and underperformed the seeding 8 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Jonathan takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #8 at 2025 Capital NE OLC in BS U15, and finishing 33rd. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Jonathan. The evidence suggests they should not have.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jonathan is not just losing locally — Jonathan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wu family travels cross-country so Jonathan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jonathan has 44 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jonathan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15, which means Jonathan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Jonathan entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 49th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Jonathan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Jonathan story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 5 titles across 6 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who living proof that past performance does not guarantee future results. But every season Jonathan comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Jonathan, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.
