That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 OBA NW OLC, where Jonathan entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (BS U13, XD U13, BD U13). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Jonathan does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Jonathan has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 1 wins means Jonathan is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jonathan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Jonathan is 0-for-20. Zero titles in 20 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Jonathan and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Jonathan needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Jonathan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jonathan's texts. The go-to partner is Daiwik Reddy (19 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U13 — we hope Daiwik Reddy has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Jonathan's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jonathan entered 3 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jonathan's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jonathan and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jonathan has shown up 9 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Jonathan has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jonathan is not just losing locally — Jonathan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Jonathan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jonathan has 35 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jonathan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U13, which means Jonathan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Jonathan entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Jonathan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Jonathan's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 1 title shine bright, but they are surrounded by 58 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Jonathan is a player who the undisputed champion of second place in Boys' Singles. Will Jonathan figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Jonathan's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

jordan_lau.roast:
Clear your schedule, because we need to talk about Jordan Lau, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Jordan has 51 career entries across 19 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Jordan saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Jordan would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times Jordan has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times Jordan has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Jordan's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jordan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 51 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 21 of them, to be precise. Jordan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jordan's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Lorin Leung: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Lorin Leung has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U15 — we hope Anson Yat Shun Fong has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Jordan has shown up 3 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jordan is not just losing locally — Jordan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lau family travels cross-country so Jordan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jordan has 33 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jordan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U15, which means Jordan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Jordan entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Jordan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

4 seasons. 51 entries. Zero titles. If this were a stock, the SEC would have delisted it by now. If this were a restaurant, the health inspector would have questions. If this were a dating profile, the app would gently suggest 'trying a different approach.' But this is junior badminton, where hope springs eternal and so does Jordan's entry form. We salute the commitment. We question the strategy. We will absolutely be back next season to see if anything changes. (It will not.)

A top-2 finish at the Pan Am Juniors puts Joshua in rarified international company.

Joshua is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior silver medalist (Boy's Singles, Mixed Doubles, 2021) • Junior Nationals champion (BD U19).

  • Ashley Yang (current partner) — currently teaming up in Mixed/XD (2 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Joshua Yuan — 1 events, 1 title together in BD
joshua_j_yang.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Joshua J Yang, from California, continues to exist, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Joshua is a Pan American Junior Championships silver medalist (Boy's Singles, Mixed Doubles), which means Joshua has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Joshua's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. But here is the thing about a silver medal: it means Joshua went all the way to an international championship and STILL did not win. Joshua represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 5 career entries and a 20% win rate, the Pan Am silver is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 5 entries worth of material.

The Joshua saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Joshua burst onto the scene with a title in BD U19 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Joshua has delivered on some of those promises: 1 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Joshua promised and what Joshua has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2020-2021 season — 1 title in 3 entries — a stretch where Joshua actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Joshua is 0-for-2. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Joshua plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Joshua is 0-for-2. Zero titles in 2 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Joshua and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Joshua needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo.

In the end, Joshua's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 1 title shine bright, but they are surrounded by 4 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Joshua is a player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. Will Joshua figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Joshua's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

With 2 career titles across 2 tournaments, this is a player who delivers on the biggest stages.

Joshua competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior champion (BS U17, BS U15, BD U15, BD U17, 2017, 2019).

In doubles, Joshua has formed effective partnerships:
  • Henry Tang (current partner) — 2 events in BD, producing 2 titles together
  • Connor Lam — 2 events in BD, best: #2

The 2018-2019 season tells a story of acceleration, with 2 titles across 1 tournaments — a clear sign that Joshua is still ascending.

Joshua has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
joshua_yang.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Joshua Yang file just landed on our desk. Joshua hails from parts unknown, currently competing at the U17 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Joshua is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist (BS U17, BS U15, BD U15, BD U17), which means Joshua has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Joshua's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. A Pan Am gold medal. International champion. The real deal. We would love to end the story right here, but unfortunately there are 6 career entries to discuss and a 33% overall win rate that suggests the Pan Am gold was the peak of a very pointy mountain with a LOT of valley on either side. Let's dig in.

The Joshua saga began at 2017 Pan Am Junior with 3 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2019 Pan Am Junior in BD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Joshua's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2018-2019 season has actually been the best yet — 2 titles in 3 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Now we arrive at the part of the program that Joshua's therapist probably knows by heart: the finals record. Brace yourself. Joshua has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2017 Pan Am Junior, where Joshua entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (BS U15, BD U15, BD U15). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Joshua does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Joshua has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 2 wins means Joshua is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

The Joshua story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 2 titles across 2 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who chokes in finals like it is a paid hobby. But every season Joshua comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Joshua, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

On the doubles front, Judy has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in XD U17 — we hope Kaicheng Ouyang has emotionally recovered.

Judy has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Judy has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Judy has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Judy showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in GS U15, which means Judy traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Judy entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Judy paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Judy keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Judy has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Judy, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

What makes Judy especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Judy has built chemistry with several doubles partners:

Judy has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning NW, NorCal, SoCal, Judy isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Judy's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
judy_zhu.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Judy Zhu, from California, continues to exist, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 12 events across 7 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 12-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Judy's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Judy keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Judy saga began at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) with 1 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

If Judy's career were a movie, the finals record would be the part where the audience starts stress-eating popcorn. Here goes. Judy has reached the finals 2 times — at 2024 Dave Freeman Jr OLC — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Judy has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Judy plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 12 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Judy has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Judy has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Judy has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Judy showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in GS U15, which means Judy traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Judy entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Judy paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Judy keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Judy has survived 2 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Judy knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.

julia_ruan.name: Julia Ruan
julia_ruan.summary:
Representing Washington, Julia Ruan broke through with a tournament title, currently competing at the U19 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 37 entries across 14 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

What makes Julia especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Julia has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 12 different players across the career:
  • Manuel Melwyn Alleluia (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Zimeng Zhou — 3 events in GD, best: #4
  • Elizabeth Hu — 4 events, 1 title together in GD, across 2 seasons

A veteran of the junior circuit, Julia has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Julia has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Julia brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
julia_ruan.roast:
Step right up and meet Julia Ruan, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Julia has 37 career entries across 14 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 3% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Julia saga began at 2021 NW CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2022 NW Oregon OLC in GD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Julia's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 14 entries — a stretch where Julia actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Julia is 0-for-2. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Julia would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Julia has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Julia has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Julia's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Julia plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Julia is 0-for-14. Zero titles in 14 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Julia and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Julia needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Julia has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Julia's texts. The go-to partner is Elizabeth Hu (4 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U17 — we hope Nicholas Volfbeyn has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Julia's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Julia has shown up 3 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Julia is not just losing locally — Julia is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ruan family travels cross-country so Julia can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Julia has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Julia showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U17, which means Julia traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Julia entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Julia paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title, 37 entries, and 36 that ended without a trophy. A player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the moments of brilliance — Julia looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Julia. We will be watching.

jun_heng_lai.name: Jun Heng Lai
jun_heng_lai.summary:
A California competitor, Jun Heng Lai is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist, earning a podium finish representing the USA against the best young players in the Western Hemisphere (Mixed Doubles). A top-4 finish at the Pan Am Juniors puts Jun in rarified international company.

Jun is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (5 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Jun competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 3 doubles titles, 9 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior bronze medalist (Mixed Doubles, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event champion (U17 XD, U17 BD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (XD U17, XD U19, BD U19) • 10 ORC titles.

Jun has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 16 different players across the career:
  • Zicheng Xu (current partner) — 6 events, 1 title together in BD/U17, spanning 3 seasons
  • Sydney Li (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD
  • Nicole Krawczyk — 3 events, 1 title together in Mixed/U19/XD

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 5 podium finishes across 2 tournaments. Jun is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Jun has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jun has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 11 seeded entries across the career, Jun consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 15 titles, Jun is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
jun_heng_lai.roast:
Clear your schedule, because we need to talk about Jun Heng Lai, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Jun is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist (Mixed Doubles, Men's Singles), which means Jun has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Jun's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. But here is the thing about a bronze medal: it means Jun went all the way to an international championship and STILL did not win. Jun represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 57 career entries and a 26% win rate, the Pan Am bronze is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 57 entries worth of material.

The Jun saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jun's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 5 titles in 17 entries — a stretch where Jun actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 6 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Now we arrive at the part of the program that Jun's therapist probably knows by heart: the finals record. Brace yourself. Jun has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2021 HPBC NW ORC, where Jun entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U15, XD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Jun does own 15 actual titles, so it is not like Jun has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 15 wins means Jun is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jun plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 3 doubles, 9 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jun can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Jun has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jun's texts. The most successful partnership is with Jasmine Yeung — 10 events, 8 titles — and honestly, Jasmine Yeung deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Jasmine Yeung a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U15 — we hope Yeening Yap has emotionally recovered.

One thing nobody can accuse Jun of is playing it safe. Jun has entered older age groups at 5 different tournaments, spanning U15, U17, U19 — which means Jun has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 13 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Jun has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jun is not just losing locally — Jun is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 26% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lai family travels cross-country so Jun can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jun has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jun showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NW ORC in BS U15, which means Jun traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 NorCal CRC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NW ORC, where Jun entered 3 events with results ranging from 1st to 65th. That is an average placement of 23rd, which is a polite way of saying Jun paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Jun story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 15 titles across 5 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who chokes in finals like it is a paid hobby. But every season Jun comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Jun, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

What makes Junjie especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

  • Derek Sun (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, across 2 seasons)

junjie_lao.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Junjie Lao, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 17 events across 8 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 17-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Junjie's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Junjie keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Junjie saga began at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) with 2 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Junjie plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 17 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Junjie has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Derek Sun (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Derek Sun has emotionally recovered.

Junjie has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Junjie has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Junjie showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13, which means Junjie traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Junjie entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Junjie paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Junjie keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Junjie has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Junjie, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

Junwoo has 43 career entries across 16 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Junwoo saga began at 2023 South ORC with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Junwoo plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Junwoo has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Junwoo's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Tanvi Jandhyala: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Tanvi Jandhyala has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Tanvi Jandhyala has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Junwoo has shown up 3 times with a best of top-49. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Junwoo is not just losing locally — Junwoo is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lee family travels cross-country so Junwoo can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Junwoo has 33 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Junwoo showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15, which means Junwoo traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Junwoo entered 3 events with results ranging from 49th to 65th. That is an average placement of 59th, which is a polite way of saying Junwoo paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Junwoo is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Junwoo the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Junwoo. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 2 titles in 15 entries — a stretch where Justin actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Justin is 0-for-12. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Boys' Doubles finals and Justin — name a more iconic tragedy. 3 trips to the championship match, and Justin has turned 1 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Justin's trophies to save time.

If Justin's career were a movie, the finals record would be the part where the audience starts stress-eating popcorn. Here goes. Justin has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 NE OLC (NVBC), where Justin entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (BS U13, XD U13, BD U13). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Justin does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Justin has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 3 wins means Justin is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Justin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. The point is, Justin can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Justin has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Justin's texts. The go-to partner is Henry Tan (10 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Niyati Tamerissi has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Justin's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Justin has shown up 6 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Justin is not just losing locally — Justin is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Dong family travels cross-country so Justin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Justin has 34 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Justin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13, which means Justin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Justin entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Justin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Justin's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 3 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 58 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Justin is a player who the undisputed champion of second place in Boys' Doubles. Will Justin figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Justin's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

justin_foh.roast:
Allow us to introduce Justin Foh, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 13 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 13-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Justin's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Justin keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Justin saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Justin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 13 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Justin has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Nicholas Foh (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in XD U15 — we hope Sandra Chen has emotionally recovered.

Justin has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Justin has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Justin has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Justin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in BS U15, which means Justin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19), where Justin entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Justin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Justin keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Justin has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Justin, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Anushka Mandadi has emotionally recovered.

The results in those older brackets have been... character-building. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Justin has built a permanent residence on that line. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Justin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 5 seasons and 28 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Justin is not most humans. Justin is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Justin the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Justin. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

justin_kan.name: Justin Kan
justin_kan.summary:
A California competitor, Justin Kan has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U15 BD) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 4 career titles across 24 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Justin is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Justin especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #3, 3 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U15 BD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals top-2 (BS U17, BD U17) • 2 ORC titles.

Justin has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Nihal Kadamba (current primary partner) — 8 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (23 total events in BD/U15/U17/U19, spanning 5 seasons)
  • Charity Lam — a trusted U15/XD partner (6 events, best: #4)
  • Samanvi Boppana — a trusted U17/XD partner (4 events, across 2 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 1 title across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Justin is still ascending.

Justin has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Justin has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 10 seeded entries across the career, Justin consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 4 titles, Justin is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
justin_kan.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Justin Kan file just landed on our desk. Justin hails from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Justin is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Justin has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Justin would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Justin, we kept reading — all 59 entries across 24 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 7% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Justin held up a trophy, there were roughly 13 other times Justin walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Justin saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2022 South Frisco ORC in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Justin's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 3 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Justin actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 6 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Boys' Doubles finals and Justin — name a more iconic tragedy. 6 trips to the championship match, and Justin has turned 3 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Justin's trophies to save time.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Justin reaches a final. Justin has reached 7 championship matches across this career. That is 7 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 7 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Justin does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Justin has never won anything. But 7 finals losses versus 4 wins means Justin is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Justin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Justin is 0-for-18. Zero titles in 18 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Justin and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Justin needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. The most successful partnership is with Nihal Kadamba — 23 events, 4 titles — and honestly, Nihal Kadamba deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Nihal Kadamba a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U17 — we hope Nihal Kadamba has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Justin has competed 5 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one.

One thing nobody can accuse Justin of is playing it safe. Justin has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U15, U17, U19 — which means Justin has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 1 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Justin has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Justin is not just losing locally — Justin is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Kan family travels cross-country so Justin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-97 finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Justin has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Justin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 97th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, which means Justin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Justin entered 2 events with results ranging from 49th to 97th. That is an average placement of 73rd, which is a polite way of saying Justin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Justin story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 4 titles across 5 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who has turned Boys' Doubles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning. But every season Justin comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Justin, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.
