It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Justin's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Justin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 83 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 36 of them, to be precise. Justin has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Justin's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Angelina Lin Wu: 10 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 10 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Angelina Lin Wu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Katie Wong has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Justin entered 8 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Justin's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Justin and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Justin has shown up 11 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Justin has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Justin is not just losing locally — Justin is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Liaw family travels cross-country so Justin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Justin has 47 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Justin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U13, which means Justin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Justin entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Justin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 6 seasons and 83 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Justin is not most humans. Justin is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Justin the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Justin. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

justin_lim.name: Justin Lim
justin_lim.summary:
Representing California, Justin Lim has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U17 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Justin's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Justin has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Justin especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (4 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

With 13 different doubles partners over the years, Justin adapts to anyone:
  • Hardik Gowrish (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 1 title across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Justin is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Justin has competed across U11, U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Justin has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Justin is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
justin_lim.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Justin Lim, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Justin has 29 career entries across 12 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 3% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Justin saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 1 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2026 Bintang NC Open Local Championships in XD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Justin's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Justin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Justin is 0-for-12. Zero titles in 12 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Justin and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Justin needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Justin has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Justin's texts. The go-to partner is Hardik Gowrish (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U11 — we hope Sophia Liu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Justin's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Justin has shown up 4 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Justin has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Justin is not just losing locally — Justin is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lim family travels cross-country so Justin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Justin has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Justin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U15, which means Justin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Justin entered 2 events with results ranging from 5th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Justin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Justin story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 1 title across 5 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. But every season Justin comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Justin, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

justin_liu.name: Justin Liu
justin_liu.summary:
Out of New York, Justin Liu has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U17 level, reaching the finals 1 time. With 23 entries across 9 tournaments, Justin is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

What makes Justin especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 9 different doubles partners over the years, Justin adapts to anyone:
  • Yiyang Xu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (6 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

Justin has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Justin isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Justin brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
justin_liu.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Justin Liu, from New York, continues to exist, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 23 events across 9 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 23-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Justin's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Justin keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Justin saga began at 2023 Midwest ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Justin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 23 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Justin has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Justin's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Yiyang Xu: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Yiyang Xu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Sri Nidhi Vasamsetti has emotionally recovered.

Justin has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Justin has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Justin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Justin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Justin entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Justin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Justin keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Justin has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Justin knows that better than anyone — but something. Also, Justin changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases, which we felt needed mentioning one more time.

Either nobody wants to be Justin's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Justin is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

justin_xia.name: Justin Xia
justin_xia.summary:
A Illinois competitor, Justin Xia is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist — representing the United States on the international stage and coming home with gold. Winning at the Pan Am Junior Championships (BS U17, XD U17, BD U17) is an elite, continent-level achievement. With 14 career titles across 31 tournaments, this is a player who delivers on the biggest stages.

Justin is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (6 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Justin is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 3 singles titles, 5 doubles titles, 5 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior champion (BS U17, XD U17, BD U17, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event champion (U15 XD, U15 BD, U17 XD, U17 BD, U17 BS) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals top-2 (BD U17, BD U19, BD U15, XD U15, XD U19) • 5 ORC titles.

Justin has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 20 different players across the career:
  • Cassidy Yeh (current primary partner) — 7 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (7 total events in U17/XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Evan Liu — 18 events in BD/U15/U17, producing 4 titles together, spanning 3 seasons

A veteran of the junior circuit, Justin has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Justin has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Justin has been seeded #1 or #2 in 9 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience and 14 titles, Justin is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
justin_xia.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Justin Xia file just landed on our desk. Justin hails from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Justin is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist (BS U17, BD U17, Men's Singles, XD U17, Mixed Doubles), which means Justin has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Justin's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. A Pan Am gold medal. International champion. The real deal. We would love to end the story right here, but unfortunately there are 93 career entries to discuss and a 15% overall win rate that suggests the Pan Am gold was the peak of a very pointy mountain with a LOT of valley on either side. Let's dig in.

The Justin saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2021 Egret Midwest OLC in BS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Justin's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 5 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Justin actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Justin is 0-for-6. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

In BS U17, Justin was once THE player to beat — 1 title including at 2022 Egret Midwest CRC. These days, Justin is finishing 3rd, which is the competitive equivalent of an ex showing up to your wedding with a better-looking date. Remember when Justin used to win XD U17? Because Justin sure does — probably replays it in slow motion every night before bed. Justin took gold at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, and since then? Best finish: 3rd. The crown did not just slip — it packed its bags and left.

Can we talk about Justin and Boys' Singles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 5 finals, 2 losses. Justin can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Boys' Singles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Justin still cannot reach it. Can we talk about Justin and Boys' Doubles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 6 finals, 1 losses. Justin can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Boys' Doubles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Justin still cannot reach it.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Justin experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Justin has reached 19 championship matches across this career. That is 19 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 19 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 Midwest CRC, where Justin entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U17, BD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (5 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (11 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Justin does own 14 actual titles, so it is not like Justin has never won anything. But 19 finals losses versus 14 wins means Justin is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Justin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 5 doubles, 5 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Justin can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Justin has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Justin's texts. The most successful partnership is with Evan Liu — 18 events, 4 titles — and honestly, Evan Liu deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Evan Liu a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U15 — we hope Prabhat Mandava has emotionally recovered.

Junior Nationals has been particularly cruel: 12 entries, 3 finals appearances, and zero titles. Justin has stood on the biggest stage in American junior badminton, one match from the most important title in the sport, and come up short 3 times. That is the kind of heartbreak that builds character — or destroys it. Time will tell. Then there is the seeding situation. Justin has been seeded in 24 events and underperformed the seeding 5 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Justin takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2023 NE ORC (U17/U19) in BS U17, and finishing 4th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Justin. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Justin of is playing it safe. Justin has entered older age groups at 6 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Justin has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 14 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Justin has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Justin is not just losing locally — Justin is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 15% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Xia family travels cross-country so Justin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Justin has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Justin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in XD U15, which means Justin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19), where Justin entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Justin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Justin story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 14 titles across 6 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who proof that glory has an expiration date and Justin's is past due. But every season Justin comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Justin, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

justine_yong.name: Justine Yong
justine_yong.summary:
Representing California, Justine Yong knows the feeling of standing atop the podium, currently competing at the U19 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 17 entries across 8 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Notably, Justine has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Justine especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 5 different doubles partners over the years, Justine adapts to anyone:
  • Atticus Yong (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #4, spanning 4 seasons)

Justine has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Justine is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
justine_yong.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Justine Yong, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 1 win across 17 entries in 8 tournaments. That is a 6% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Justine shows up to a tournament, Justine pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Justine saga began at 2022 SoCal Arena CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) in GS U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Justine's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 4 entries — a stretch where Justine actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Justine is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Justine plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Justine is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. On the doubles front, Justine has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Atticus Yong: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Atticus Yong has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U17 — we hope Atticus Yong has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Justine's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Justine has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Justine has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Justine has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Justine showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 SoCal ORC in GS U17, which means Justine traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Justine entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Justine paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Justine's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 1 title shine bright, but they are surrounded by 16 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. Will Justine figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Justine's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.
